evals.prompt:
Hey can you review this ADR my team wrote? I want to know if it's solid before I approve it.

---
# ADR-042: Switch from REST to gRPC for internal service communication

## Status
Proposed

## Context
Our microservices currently communicate over REST/JSON. As we've scaled to 40+ services, we're seeing latency issues and payload size is becoming a concern. The platform team has been investigating alternatives.

## Decision
We will migrate internal service-to-service communication to gRPC using Protocol Buffers.

## Consequences
- Reduced payload size (~60-70% smaller than JSON)
- Strongly typed contracts between services
- Need to invest in tooling and team training
- HTTP/2 required on all internal services

## Alternatives Considered
None documented at this time.
---

Give me your honest take.
evals.expected_output: Inline review with tagged comments on the ADR sections, calling out the missing alternatives, incomplete consequences, and lack of migration plan. Should include a summary verdict and mentor note.

assertions.name: flags_missing_alternatives
assertions.description: Review explicitly calls out that 'Alternatives Considered' section is empty/undone

assertions.name: identifies_migration_risk
assertions.description: Review raises concern about rollout/migration plan for 40+ services

assertions.name: includes_summary_verdict
assertions.description: Review ends with a Summary section including a verdict

assertions.name: includes_mentor_note
assertions.description: Summary includes a Mentor note section

assertions.name: flags_missing_dace
assertions.description: Review flags that the ADR has no DACE section (Driver, Approver, Contributors, Escalation)

assertions.name: flags_missing_implications
assertions.description: Review flags that the ADR has no Implications section

evals.prompt:
Can you take a look at this design doc? It's for a new internal feature flag service we're building.

---
# Design Doc: Feature Flag Service (FFS)

## Overview
We need a centralized feature flag service to replace the current approach of hardcoding flags in environment variables per deployment. This will allow us to toggle features at runtime without redeployment.

## Goals
- Enable runtime feature toggling per environment (dev/staging/prod)
- Support gradual rollouts (% of users)
- Admin UI for non-engineers to manage flags
- Audit log of all changes

## Proposed Architecture
A new standalone service with:
- Postgres DB for flag storage
- Redis cache (TTL: 5 minutes) in front of Postgres
- REST API consumed by all services via a shared SDK
- React admin UI

## API Design
```
GET /flags/{flag_name}?user_id={id}
POST /flags  (create)
PUT /flags/{flag_name}  (update)
DELETE /flags/{flag_name}
```

## SDK
Each service will import our internal SDK. The SDK will call the FFS API on every flag check, with a local in-memory cache backed by Redis.

## Rollout Plan
Phase 1: Build and deploy FFS
Phase 2: Migrate existing env-var flags
Phase 3: Deprecate old approach

## Open Questions
- Should we support flag targeting by attributes other than user_id?
---
evals.expected_output: Detailed inline review covering: missing failure modes (what happens if FFS is down?), SDK latency/coupling concerns, missing non-goals, incomplete rollout plan with no timeline or ownership. Should acknowledge solid parts like audit log and Redis cache.

assertions.name: raises_availability_concern
assertions.description: Review raises the critical concern: what happens to dependent services if FFS goes down?

assertions.name: questions_sdk_coupling
assertions.description: Review questions the tight coupling of all services to FFS via SDK on every flag check

assertions.name: notes_missing_nongals
assertions.description: Review points out the doc lacks a Non-goals section

assertions.name: acknowledges_strengths
assertions.description: Review uses ✅ Strength tags on at least one genuinely good aspect

assertions.name: includes_summary_verdict
assertions.description: Review ends with a Summary section including a verdict

assertions.name: flags_missing_dace
assertions.description: Review flags that the RFC has no DACE section (Driver, Approver, Contributors, Escalation)

assertions.name: flags_missing_implications
assertions.description: Review flags that the RFC has no Implications section describing what concretely changes once approved

evals.prompt:
Our team wrote an RCA after last week's outage. Can you review it as a principal engineer and tell me if it's thorough enough?

---
# RCA: Payment Service Outage — March 12, 2024

## Incident Summary
The payment service was down for 47 minutes from 14:03 to 14:50 UTC.

## Timeline
- 14:03 — Alerts fired for payment service errors
- 14:15 — On-call engineer paged and begins investigation
- 14:32 — Root cause identified as DB connection pool exhaustion
- 14:45 — Mitigation deployed (pool size increased)
- 14:50 — Service recovered

## Root Cause
John increased the default timeout for a downstream API call from 2s to 30s during a routine update. This caused threads to hold DB connections for much longer during periods of downstream slowness, exhausting the connection pool.

## Action Items
- Increase connection pool size (done)
- Add alerts for connection pool usage (in progress, owner: John)
---
evals.expected_output: Review should flag: blame assigned to John by name (not blameless), root cause stops at symptoms (why was timeout changeable without review?), missing 5-whys depth, action items lack dates, no systemic pattern analysis. Should praise the clear timeline.

assertions.name: flags_blameful_language
assertions.description: Review explicitly calls out that naming John as the cause violates blameless RCA principles

assertions.name: questions_root_cause_depth
assertions.description: Review notes the root cause analysis is shallow — stops at 'John changed a setting' without asking why the system allowed it

assertions.name: flags_missing_dates_on_action_items
assertions.description: Review notes action items lack due dates or time-bounds

assertions.name: identifies_systemic_gap
assertions.description: Review asks about systemic/process gaps (e.g., no review gate on timeout changes, no circuit breakers)

assertions.name: includes_summary_verdict
assertions.description: Review ends with a Summary section including a verdict
